erroneous_event:
Then, away from the lights, he changed.

“It’s a show,” I told him. “They want a story.”

He shook his head. “No. I know you don’t believe that.”
<error>“Six weeks ago you would’ve given me everything,” he said softly.</error>

He said he’d been scared—of going home and never seeing me again, of me outgrowing him, of winning and being stuck together, of actually liking me. Then he looked at me and waited.

I felt it break open inside me—the thing I’d been pressing flat for weeks.

“It’s not just a contract,” I whispered.

“It’s not just a contract,” he echoed, smiling.

He kissed me then—with no audience, no mentor pulling strings, no glittering gown or timed music. My bag thudded to the floor, his hand found my cheek, and the moon lit the quiet hallway.

It was just us.

And for once, it was real.
story:
The final note faded, the crowd roared, and Noah and I kissed on cue—my mic tilted just enough to pretend at privacy. The judges stood, raving about our chemistry and flawless harmonies. Our mentor, Samuel, beamed. His plan had worked: two talented singers packaged as a love story.

According to the show, we’d met at auditions, got paired after Bootcamp, and fallen in love on national TV. It would have been perfect—if any of it were true.

Backstage, we shed the sequins for sweats. Noah joked about being my “perfect, charming, super handsome boyfriend,” echoing Samuel’s pitch: I was the shy girl with the secret voice; he was the charismatic boyfriend who pulled me into the spotlight. I hated the story, but it kept us at the top week after week. I told myself it was worth it if it got me a record deal.

We’d tried to make the act real once. In the piano room—the only quiet corner in the house—we traded questions under the lid’s shadow. Favorite color. Favorite food. Favorite place. Favorite movie. We laughed too loud, too long, until someone told us to keep it down. Then we whispered until it felt like we’d known each other forever.
That was the first time he kissed me. For a moment, it wasn’t part of the show.

But the performance swallowed everything.

After one of our strongest nights, we slipped into the empty hallway. Noah followed me to my door, my bag still slung over his shoulder, eyes on the floor.

“How do you do it?” he asked. “How do you pretend this doesn’t affect you?”

“We’re acting,” I said, turning my head away. “It’s a job. This is how we get the contract.”

A brief time silence. Then he said he couldn’t separate it. He wanted to keep it strictly professional. We’d be in love for the cameras—and nowhere else.

I was hurt, then angry, then numb. I told myself I could block it out. A song was just a song. A kiss was just choreography. If I focused on the prize, the rest wouldn’t matter.
And for a while, it didn’t. We sang, smiled, and sold the story the audience wanted.

Weeks passed.
Six weeks ago, I would’ve given that boy everything.
Four weeks ago, I hated him for letting go so easily.
Two weeks ago, I finally accepted that the whole thing was a game.

Then, away from the lights, he changed.

“It’s a show,” I told him. “They want a story.”

He shook his head. “No. I know you don’t believe that.” “Six weeks ago you would’ve given me everything,” he said softly. “Four weeks ago you hated me for letting go. Two weeks ago you decided it was all a game.”

He said he’d been scared—of going home and never seeing me again, of me outgrowing him, of winning and being stuck together, of actually liking me. Then he looked at me and waited.

I felt it break open inside me—the thing I’d been pressing flat for weeks.

“It’s not just a contract,” I whispered.

“It’s not just a contract,” he echoed, smiling.

He kissed me then—with no audience, no mentor pulling strings, no glittering gown or timed music. My bag thudded to the floor, his hand found my cheek, and the moon lit the quiet hallway.

It was just us.

And for once, it was real.
